This probability interpretation (Born interpretation) means only the probability of outcomes can be predicted, not individual events.

Quantum tunnelling: a particle can penetrate and pass through a potential energy barrier even when its kinetic energy is less than the barrier height — classically forbidden but quantum-mechanically allowed because the wavefunction has a non-zero amplitude inside and beyond the barrier. Applications: scanning tunnelling microscopy (STM) — tunnelling current between tip and surface maps atomic topography; alpha decay — alpha particles tunnel through the Coulomb barrier of the nucleus despite lacking sufficient classical energy to escape.

The uncertainty principle has profound consequences: electrons cannot exist inside the nucleus (their confinement to ~$10^{-14}$ m would imply kinetic energies far exceeding observed nuclear binding energies); atoms cannot collapse (electrons in the ground state are prevented from spiralling into the nucleus by the minimum uncertainty in momentum/kinetic energy). Wave-particle duality is confirmed by electron diffraction — the Davisson–Germer experiment (1927) showed electron beams diffracting off a nickel crystal with a pattern consistent with de Broglie wavelengths — and the single-electron double-slit experiment, where individual electrons build up an interference pattern over time.

quiz.question: Describe three features of the photoelectric effect that classical wave theory cannot explain, and explain how the photon model accounts for each.
quiz.markscheme: (1) Threshold frequency: classical theory predicts any frequency (given enough intensity) should eject electrons. Photon model: only photons with $E = hf \geq \varphi$ (work function) have enough energy to eject one electron; below $f_0 = \varphi/h$, no single photon has sufficient energy. (2) Kinetic energy independent of intensity: classical theory predicts higher intensity should give electrons more energy. Photon model: intensity determines the number of photons per second (more electrons ejected), not each photon's energy; $E_k = hf - \varphi$ depends only on frequency. (3) Instantaneous emission: classical theory predicts a time delay (energy must accumulate). Photon model: a single photon immediately transfers all its energy to one electron — no accumulation needed.

quiz.question: Light of wavelength $250$ nm hits a metal surface with work function $3.5$ eV. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons and the stopping potential. ($h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}$ J s, $c = 3.0 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$)
quiz.markscheme: Photon energy: $E = \tfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \tfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^8}{250 \times 10^{-9}} = \tfrac{1.989 \times 10^{-25}}{2.50 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.956 \times 10^{-19} J = \tfrac{7.956 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} eV = 4.97 eV. Work function \varphi = 3.5 eV. E_{k,\max} = hf - \varphi = 4.97 - 3.5 = 1.47 eV = 1.47 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 2.35 \times 10^{-19} J. Stopping potential: eV_s = E_{k,\max}\RightarrowV_s = 1.47 V \approx 1.5$ V.

quiz.question: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through $100$ V. ($m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}$ J s)
quiz.markscheme: Energy gained: $E_k = eV = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100 = 1.6 \times 10^{-17}$ J. Speed: $v = \sqrt{\tfrac{2E_k}{m}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-17}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{3.513 \times 10^{13}} = 5.927 \times 10^{6} m s^{-1}. Momentum: p = mv = 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.927 \times 10^{6} = 5.400 \times 10^{-24} kg m s^{-1}. de Broglie wavelength: \lambda = h/p = \tfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{5.400 \times 10^{-24}} = 1.228 \times 10^{-10} m \approx 0.12 nm. This is comparable to atomic spacings in crystals (~0.2$ nm), explaining why electrons produce diffraction patterns with crystal targets.

quiz.question: State the Heisenberg uncertainty principle and apply it to estimate the minimum kinetic energy of an electron confined to a nucleus of diameter $10$ fm.
quiz.markscheme: Uncertainty principle: $\Delta x\, \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ ($\hbar = h/(2\pi) = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J s). If electron confined to $\Delta x = 10 \times 10^{-15}$ m $= 10^{-14}$ m: $\Delta p \geq \tfrac{\hbar}{2\Delta x} = \tfrac{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-14}} = 5.28 \times 10^{-21} kg m s^{-1}. Minimum kinetic energy: E_k = \tfrac{(\Delta p)^2}{2m_e} = \tfrac{(5.28 \times 10^{-21})^2}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = \tfrac{2.79 \times 10^{-41}}{1.82 \times 10^{-30}} = 1.53 \times 10^{-11} J \approx 95$ MeV. This huge energy far exceeds what nuclear forces could provide, showing electrons cannot exist in the nucleus — validating the neutron model over the proton-electron nucleus model.

quiz.question: Describe the quantum tunnelling effect and explain how it enables alpha decay despite the alpha particle lacking sufficient energy to escape the nucleus classically.
quiz.markscheme: Quantum tunnelling: a quantum particle (described by a wavefunction) can penetrate a potential energy barrier even when its total energy is less than the barrier height. The wavefunction does not drop to zero at the barrier; it decays exponentially inside but has a non-zero amplitude on the other side. The probability of transmission depends on the barrier height and width. In alpha decay: the alpha particle exists as a cluster within the nucleus, trapped by the strong nuclear force (attractive well) surrounded by a Coulomb repulsion barrier (the electrostatic repulsion between the alpha and remaining nucleus). Classically, the alpha cannot escape. Quantum mechanically, the wavefunction tunnels through the Coulomb barrier with a small but non-zero probability. Over time (half-life depends on tunnelling probability), the alpha particle escapes. Thin, low barriers give higher tunnelling probability and shorter half-lives; thick, high barriers give longer half-lives.

quiz.question: Describe the double-slit experiment performed with single electrons and explain what it implies about the nature of quantum objects.
quiz.markscheme: In the electron double-slit experiment, a beam of electrons (or even individual electrons fired one at a time) passes through two narrow slits and lands on a detector screen. Individual electrons create single, localized flashes on the screen (particle-like). But after many electrons have been recorded, an interference pattern of alternating bands builds up — identical to that seen with light waves. Crucially, this pattern appears even when electrons are sent one at a time (ruling out electron–electron interaction). This means each electron must pass through both slits simultaneously as a wave (superposition of two paths) and interfere with itself. The probability of landing at any point is given by $|\psi|^2$, where $\psi$ is the wavefunction. If a 'which-path' detector is placed at the slits, the interference pattern disappears — the act of measurement collapses the wavefunction. This illustrates wave–particle duality and the probabilistic, non-deterministic nature of quantum mechanics.

title: Radioactive decay
objectives: Describe the properties of alpha ($\alpha$), beta-minus ($\beta^-$), beta-plus ($\beta^+$), and gamma ($\gamma$) radiations, including penetrating power and ionising ability.
Write balanced nuclear equations for alpha, beta-minus, beta-plus, and gamma decay.
Define the decay constant $\lambda$ and apply the exponential decay law: $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
Define half-life $T_{1/2}$ and derive the relationship $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$.
Explain radioactive decay series and describe safety precautions for handling radioactive sources.
Apply radioactive decay to carbon dating and other radiometric dating techniques.
summary:
Radioactive decay is the spontaneous and random disintegration of an unstable nucleus to reach a more stable configuration. It is spontaneous: it occurs without any external trigger and is unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemical state. It is random: it is impossible to predict which particular nucleus will decay next or when; only probabilities can be stated. Three principal decay types: Alpha ($\alpha$) decay — emission of a helium-4 nucleus (${}^4_2\mathrm{He}$), charge $+2e$, mass number 4; highly ionising (~$10\,000$ ion pairs per mm in air); stopped by a few cm of air or a sheet of paper; most dangerous if the source is inside the body. Beta-minus ($\beta^-$) decay — a neutron converts to a proton: $\mathrm{n} \to \mathrm{p} + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$; emits an electron and an antineutrino; charge $-e$; moderately ionising; stopped by ~$3$ mm of aluminium. Beta-plus ($\beta^+$) decay — a proton converts to a neutron: $\mathrm{p} \to \mathrm{n} + e^+ + \nu_e$; emits a positron and a neutrino; stopped by ~mm of material; positron annihilates with an electron producing two gamma photons. Gamma ($\gamma$) radiation — high-energy electromagnetic photons emitted when a nucleus de-excites after alpha or beta decay; no charge or mass; weakly ionising; stopped (attenuated) by many cm of lead or thick concrete; most hazardous from external sources.

Nuclear equations conserve mass number A (top number) and atomic number Z (bottom number). Alpha decay: ${}^A_Z\mathrm{X} \to {}^{A-4}_{Z-2}\mathrm{Y} + {}^4_2\mathrm{He}$. Beta-minus: ${}^A_Z\mathrm{X} \to {}^A_{Z+1}\mathrm{Y} + {}^0_{-1}e + \bar{\nu}_e$. Beta-plus: ${}^A_Z\mathrm{X} \to {}^A_{Z-1}\mathrm{Y} + {}^0_{+1}e + \nu_e$. Gamma emission: ${}^A_Z\mathrm{X}^* \to {}^A_Z\mathrm{X} + \gamma$ (A and Z unchanged). The antineutrino in $\beta^-$ (and neutrino in $\beta^+$) explains why beta electrons are emitted with a continuous spectrum of kinetic energies, rather than a discrete value (the neutrino/antineutrino carries the remainder of the available energy).

The decay law: each nucleus has the same probability $\lambda$ (the decay constant, s$^{-1}$) of decaying per unit time, independent of age. This gives the differential equation $\tfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$, with solution $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. Activity $A = \left|\tfrac{dN}{dt}\right| = \lambda N = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} (becquerel, Bq = 1 decay s^{-1}$). Half-life $T_{1/2} is defined as the time for N (or A) to halve: T_{1/2} = \tfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \tfrac{0.693}{\lambda}. After n$ half-lives: $N = N_0 \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n. The mean lifetime \tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/\ln 2 \approx 1.44\, T_{1/2}$ (the average time a nucleus survives before decaying).

Background radiation is always present from natural sources (cosmic rays, radon gas from rocks, potassium-40 in food and body, radiation from building materials) and must be subtracted from measured count rates in experiments. The corrected count rate = measured rate $-$ background rate.

Radioactive decay series: a heavy nucleus such as ${}^{238}_{92}\mathrm{U}$ undergoes a sequence of alpha and beta decays, passing through many intermediate isotopes, until a stable lead isotope (${}^{206}_{82}\mathrm{Pb}$) is reached. This U-238 series has 14 steps. Carbon-14 dating exploits the constant ratio of ${}^{14}\mathrm{C}/{}^{12}\mathrm{C}$ maintained in living organisms by cosmic ray production of ${}^{14}\mathrm{C}$ (from ${}^{14}\mathrm{N} + \mathrm{n} \to {}^{14}\mathrm{C} + \mathrm{p}$) and absorption through photosynthesis and food chains. When an organism dies, ${}^{14}\mathrm{C}$ ($T_{1/2} = 5730$ yr) decays without replenishment; measuring the residual ${}^{14}\mathrm{C}$ activity and comparing to a living reference gives the age. Useful range: ~$500$–$50\,000$ yr. Potassium-40 ($T_{1/2} = 1.3 \times 10^9$ yr) and uranium-238 ($T_{1/2} = 4.47 \times 10^9$ yr) are used for geological dating of rocks.

quiz.question: Compare alpha, beta, and gamma radiation in terms of charge, mass, ionising ability, and penetrating power.
quiz.markscheme: Alpha ($\alpha$): charge $+2e$, mass number 4 (helium nucleus), highest ionising ability (creates about $10\,000$ ion pairs per mm in air) but lowest penetrating power (stopped by ~$5$ cm of air or a sheet of paper). Beta ($\beta^-$): charge $-e$, mass ~$\tfrac{1}{2000}$ of proton, moderate ionisation, stopped by ~$3$ mm of aluminium. Gamma ($\gamma$): no charge, no mass, weakest ionisation but highest penetrating power (attenuated, never fully stopped, by many cm of lead or concrete). Alpha is most dangerous if source is inside the body; gamma is most dangerous from external sources.

quiz.question: Write balanced nuclear equations for (a) alpha decay of uranium-238 and (b) beta-minus decay of carbon-14.
quiz.markscheme: (a) Alpha decay of ${}^{238}_{92}\mathrm{U}$: ${}^{238}_{92}\mathrm{U} \to {}^{234}_{90}\mathrm{Th} + {}^{4}_{2}\mathrm{He}$. Check: A: $238 = 234 + 4$ ✓; Z: $92 = 90 + 2$ ✓. Thorium-234 is the daughter. (b) Beta-minus decay of ${}^{14}_{6}\mathrm{C}$: ${}^{14}_{6}\mathrm{C} \to {}^{14}_{7}\mathrm{N} + {}^{0}_{-1}e + \bar{\nu}_e$. Check: A: $14 = 14 + 0$ ✓; Z: $6 = 7 - 1$ ✓. Nitrogen-14 is the daughter. (The antineutrino $\bar{\nu}_e$ is also emitted but does not affect A or Z.)

quiz.question: A radioactive source has an initial activity of $4000$ Bq and a half-life of $8.0$ days. Calculate the activity after $24$ days and the decay constant.
quiz.markscheme: Number of half-lives: $n = t/T_{1/2} = 24/8.0 = 3$. Activity after n half-lives: $A = A_0 \times \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n = 4000 \times \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^3 = 4000/8 = 500 Bq. Alternatively: \lambda = \tfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \tfrac{0.693}{8.0 \times 86\,400} = \tfrac{0.693}{691\,200} = 1.002 \times 10^{-6} s^{-1}. A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} = 4000 \times e^{-1.002 \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 86400} = 4000 \times e^{-2.078} = 4000 \times 0.125 = 500$ Bq ✓.

quiz.question: Derive the relationship between half-life $T_{1/2}$ and decay constant $\lambda$.
quiz.markscheme: The decay law is $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. The half-life $T_{1/2}$ is defined as the time when $N = N_0/2$. Substituting: $\tfrac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$. Dividing both sides by $N_0$: $\tfrac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$. Taking natural log: $\ln\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}\Rightarrow-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}\RightarrowT_{1/2} = \tfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \tfrac{0.693}{\lambda}. So \lambda = 0.693/T_{1/2}$. A larger decay constant means shorter half-life (faster decay); a smaller decay constant means longer half-life (slower decay).

quiz.question: A wooden artefact has a ${}^{14}\mathrm{C}$ activity of 15% of that expected from a living tree. Estimate its age. ($T_{1/2}$ of ${}^{14}\mathrm{C} = 5730$ years)
quiz.markscheme: Using $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}\Rightarrow0.15 A_0 = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}\Rightarrow0.15 = e^{-\lambda t}. Taking ln: \ln(0.15) = -\lambda t\Rightarrow-1.897 = -\lambda t. \lambda = \tfrac{0.693}{5730} = 1.21 \times 10^{-4} yr^{-1}. t = \tfrac{1.897}{1.21 \times 10^{-4}} = 15\,677 yr \approx 15\,700 years. The artefact is approximately 15\,700 years old. Note: carbon dating assumes the atmospheric {}^{14}\mathrm{C}/{}^{12}\mathrm{C}$ ratio has been approximately constant, which requires calibration against tree rings (dendrochronology) for precision.

quiz.question: Explain why radioactive decay is described as 'random and spontaneous' and describe one safety precaution appropriate for handling a gamma source.
quiz.markscheme: Random: it is impossible to predict which particular nucleus in a sample will decay next, or when. Each nucleus has the same probability of decaying per unit time (described by $\lambda$), regardless of its history — decay follows a Poisson distribution; the actual count rate fluctuates around the mean. Spontaneous: decay occurs without any external trigger; it is unaffected by temperature, pressure, chemical environment, or electromagnetic fields. Safety for gamma source: maximise distance from the source (intensity $\propto 1/d^2$ — inverse square law; doubling distance reduces intensity by factor 4); minimise exposure time; use lead shielding (many cm of lead significantly attenuates gamma); use remote handling tools (tongs, not hands); monitor dose with a Geiger counter or dosimeter.

title: Fission
objectives: Describe nuclear fission and explain why heavy nuclei ($A > {\sim}90$) can release energy by fission.
Explain the chain reaction in a fission reactor and the role of critical mass.
Describe the components of a nuclear fission reactor: fuel, moderator, control rods, coolant, and shielding.
Calculate the energy released in a fission reaction using $E = mc^2$ and binding energy per nucleon data.
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of nuclear fission as an energy source compared to fossil fuels.
Explain the problem of radioactive waste and outline methods for its safe disposal.
summary:
Nuclear fission is the splitting of a heavy nucleus (typically uranium-235 or plutonium-239) into two smaller daughter nuclei (fission fragments) plus typically two or three fast neutrons and a large amount of energy, usually as kinetic energy of the fragments and gamma radiation. Fission is energetically favourable for heavy nuclei because those nuclei sit on the right-hand side of the binding energy per nucleon curve, where $\mathrm{BE}/A \approx 7.6$ MeV; the fission products sit near the peak (around iron, $\mathrm{BE}/A \approx 8.8$ MeV). Because the products are more tightly bound than the original nucleus, mass is converted to energy: $\Delta E = \Delta m \times c^2$. A typical fission of U-235 releases approximately $200$ MeV per event, compared with a few eV per atom in a chemical reaction — roughly 50 million times more energy per kg of fuel.

A chain reaction occurs when each fission event releases neutrons that can trigger further fissions. For a self-sustaining (critical) reaction, the average number of neutrons that trigger a new fission per fission event (the multiplication factor k) must equal 1. If $k < 1$ (sub-critical), the reaction dies out; if $k > 1$ (super-critical), the reaction grows exponentially (as in a bomb). The minimum mass of fissile material required for a self-sustaining chain reaction is the critical mass, which depends on geometry, purity, and the presence of a reflector. In a reactor, k is kept at exactly 1 by controlling the neutron population.

A nuclear fission reactor contains several key components. Fuel rods contain enriched uranium (typically 3–5% U-235, compared with 0.7% in natural uranium) or plutonium. The moderator (often water, heavy water, or graphite) slows fast neutrons to thermal (slow) neutrons, which are far more likely to be captured by U-235 and cause fission; slowing is achieved through elastic collisions with nuclei of similar mass. Control rods (boron or cadmium) absorb neutrons; inserting them deeper into the core reduces k and lowers power output; withdrawing them raises power. In an emergency, control rods are fully inserted to shut down the reactor (SCRAM). The coolant (water, CO$_2$, or liquid sodium) carries heat away from the core to a heat exchanger and turbine. Thick concrete and steel shielding around the reactor absorbs gamma radiation and fast neutrons, protecting workers and the public.

The energy released in fission is calculated from the mass defect: $\Delta m = m(\text{reactants}) - m(\text{products})$, where masses are in atomic mass units (u; $1\,u = 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$). Then $\Delta E = \Delta m \times c^2 = \Delta m \times 931.5$ MeV. For example, for ${}^{235}_{92}\mathrm{U} + {}^{1}_{0}\mathrm{n} \to {}^{141}_{56}\mathrm{Ba} + {}^{92}_{36}\mathrm{Kr} + 3{}^{1}_{0}\mathrm{n}, the mass defect is approximately 0.186\,u, giving \Delta E \approx 0.186 \times 931.5 \approx 173$ MeV. Additional energy comes from the kinetic energy of the neutrons and gamma emission, totalling ~$200$ MeV.

Advantages of nuclear fission: very high energy density (millions of times more than fossil fuels per kg), no direct CO$_2$ emissions during operation, reliable base-load power, and relatively small land area required. Disadvantages: radioactive waste remains hazardous for thousands of years; risk of catastrophic accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima); high construction and decommissioning costs; uranium mining has environmental impacts; proliferation risk of fissile material for weapons. Radioactive waste is classified as low-level, intermediate-level, or high-level. High-level waste (spent fuel rods) must be stored in cooling ponds initially, then vitrified (encased in glass), and eventually placed in deep geological repositories (DGR) in stable rock formations.

quiz.question: Explain why uranium-235 can release energy when it undergoes fission, using the binding energy per nucleon curve.
quiz.markscheme: The binding energy per nucleon ($\mathrm{BE}/A$) curve shows that heavy nuclei such as U-235 have $\mathrm{BE}/A \approx 7.6$ MeV, which is less than the $\mathrm{BE}/A$ of their fission products (typically in the range $A \approx 90$–$140$, where $\mathrm{BE}/A \approx 8.4$–$8.8$ MeV, near the peak at iron). Because the products are more tightly bound (higher $\mathrm{BE}/A$), the total nuclear binding energy of the system increases. This increase in binding energy is released as the mass defect: $\Delta E = \Delta m \times c^2$, where $\Delta m = m(\text{reactants}) - m(\text{products})$. A typical fission releases approximately $200$ MeV per event.

quiz.question: Explain the role of the moderator and control rods in a nuclear fission reactor.
quiz.markscheme: Moderator: Fission of U-235 produces fast neutrons (kinetic energy ~$1$ MeV), which have a low probability of causing further fissions of U-235. The moderator (e.g. water, graphite) slows these neutrons to thermal energies (~$0.025$ eV) through repeated elastic collisions with nuclei of similar mass (most effective when the mass of moderator nuclei $\approx$ mass of neutron). Slow (thermal) neutrons have a much higher cross-section for absorption by U-235, sustaining the chain reaction efficiently. Control rods: Made of neutron-absorbing material (boron or cadmium), they are inserted into the reactor core to absorb excess neutrons. Inserting control rods reduces the neutron population and decreases the reaction rate (reduces k toward $< 1$); withdrawing them allows $k \to 1$ (criticality). Full insertion shuts down the reactor in an emergency.

quiz.question: A fission reaction produces a mass defect of $0.190\,u$. Calculate the energy released in MeV and in joules. ($1\,u = 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$; $1$ MeV $= 1.60 \times 10^{-13}$ J)
quiz.markscheme: Energy in MeV: $\Delta E = \Delta m \times 931.5 = 0.190 \times 931.5 = 176.985 \approx 177$ MeV. Energy in joules: $\Delta E = 177 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-13} = 2.83 \times 10^{-11}$ J. This illustrates the enormous energy density of nuclear fuel; $1$ kg of U-235 (~$2.56 \times 10^{24}$ atoms) releases on the order of $8 \times 10^{13}$ J, compared with ~$3 \times 10^7$ J per kg for coal.

quiz.question: Discuss two advantages and two disadvantages of using nuclear fission to generate electricity, compared with burning fossil fuels.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages: (1) Very high energy density — nuclear fuel releases millions of times more energy per kg than fossil fuels, so fuel requirements are tiny. (2) Zero CO$_2$ emissions during reactor operation — no greenhouse gases produced directly, unlike combustion of coal, oil, or gas. Disadvantages: (1) Radioactive waste — spent fuel rods contain highly radioactive isotopes with half-lives of thousands of years; storage in deep geological repositories is costly, and failure could contaminate groundwater. (2) Risk of serious accident — failure of cooling systems can cause core meltdown (as at Fukushima 2011), releasing radioactive material over a wide area, with long-term health and environmental consequences. Any two well-explained points from each side merit full marks.

quiz.question: Explain what is meant by critical mass and describe the condition for a self-sustaining chain reaction.
quiz.markscheme: The multiplication factor k is the average number of neutrons from each fission event that go on to produce a further fission. For a self-sustaining chain reaction, $k = 1$ (critical condition): on average exactly one neutron per fission causes a new fission, maintaining a constant reaction rate. If $k < 1$ (sub-critical), the chain reaction dies; if $k > 1$ (super-critical), the number of fissions grows exponentially. Critical mass is the minimum mass of fissile material needed to achieve $k = 1$ for a given geometry and purity. Below the critical mass, too many neutrons escape from the surface before causing fission; above it, enough are retained within the material to sustain the chain. Factors affecting critical mass: enrichment of fissile material, geometry (sphere has minimum surface-to-volume ratio), presence of a neutron reflector.

quiz.question: Outline the problem of high-level nuclear waste disposal and describe one long-term strategy for managing it.
quiz.markscheme: High-level nuclear waste (HLW) consists mainly of spent fuel rods and liquid waste from reprocessing. It contains fission products and actinides (e.g. plutonium, americium) with half-lives ranging from years to millions of years. HLW is intensely radioactive and thermally hot; it must be isolated from the biosphere until it decays to safe levels (potentially $10\,000+$ years). Strategy: Deep Geological Repository (DGR) — HLW is first stored in cooling ponds for several years, then vitrified (mixed with molten glass and solidified into glass blocks), which immobilizes the radioactive material. The glass blocks are then sealed in corrosion-resistant metal canisters and placed in tunnels or chambers drilled $300$–$1000$ m deep into stable, impermeable rock (e.g. granite, clay). The tunnels are backfilled with bentonite clay (absorbs water, swells to seal gaps). The repository provides multiple barriers (engineered + geological) to prevent migration of radionuclides into groundwater.

title: Fusion and stars
objectives: Describe nuclear fusion and explain why light nuclei ($A < {\sim}56$) can release energy by fusion.
Explain the conditions required for nuclear fusion and the challenges of achieving controlled fusion on Earth.
Describe the proton–proton chain as the main energy source in stars like the Sun.
Apply the Hertzsprung–Russell diagram to describe the life cycle of stars of different masses.
Calculate the energy released in a fusion reaction using $E = mc^2$ and binding energy per nucleon data.
Discuss nuclear fusion as a potential future energy source, including the role of tokamak reactors.
summary:
Nuclear fusion is the combining of two light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus. Fusion releases energy when the product nucleus has a higher binding energy per nucleon ($\mathrm{BE}/A$) than the reactants — this is the case for nuclei with A less than about 56 (the iron peak). The most energetically favourable terrestrial fusion reaction is between deuterium (${}^2\mathrm{H}$) and tritium (${}^3\mathrm{H}$): ${}^2_1\mathrm{H} + {}^3_1\mathrm{H} \to {}^4_2\mathrm{He} + {}^1_0\mathrm{n} + 17.6$ MeV. The helium-4 nucleus carries ~$3.5 MeV and the neutron ~14.1$ MeV. This reaction has the highest cross-section at achievable temperatures.

Fusion requires extremely high temperatures ($T \geq 10^8$ K, i.e. ~$10$ keV) to give nuclei enough kinetic energy to overcome the Coulomb (electrostatic) repulsion barrier between their positive charges and approach within the range of the strong nuclear force (~$10^{-15}$ m). In addition, the plasma must be confined at sufficient density for long enough (Lawson criterion: $n \tau_E \geq {\sim}10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$ s for D–T fusion). In stars, gravitational confinement at the core provides both the pressure and temperature needed. On Earth, magnetic confinement (tokamak reactors, using strong toroidal magnetic fields to contain the plasma) and inertial confinement (laser implosion of fuel pellets) are the main approaches. The ITER project in France is the world's largest experimental tokamak, aiming to demonstrate $Q > 1$ (more fusion energy out than heating energy in).

In stars with masses similar to the Sun, the dominant energy-producing process is the proton–proton (pp) chain. In three steps: (1) $\mathrm{p} + \mathrm{p} \to {}^2_1\mathrm{H} + e^+ + \nu_e$ (two protons fuse to form deuterium, emitting a positron and a neutrino); (2) ${}^2_1\mathrm{H} + \mathrm{p} \to {}^3_2\mathrm{He} + \gamma$; (3) ${}^3_2\mathrm{He} + {}^3_2\mathrm{He} \to {}^4_2\mathrm{He} + 2\mathrm{p}$. The net result is $4{}^1_1\mathrm{H} \to {}^4_2\mathrm{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + 2\gamma$, releasing approximately $26.7$ MeV per helium-4 nucleus produced. The Sun converts approximately $4 \times 10^9$ kg of mass into energy per second ($E = mc^2$), producing luminosity $L_\odot \approx 3.85 \times 10^{26}$ W. In more massive stars (main sequence: $M > {\sim}1.3\,M_\odot$), the CNO cycle dominates.

The Hertzsprung–Russell (HR) diagram plots stellar luminosity (L, in solar luminosities or absolute magnitude) against surface temperature ($T_{\text{eff}}$, in kelvin — note the x-axis runs right to left, from hot blue stars to cool red stars). Stars spend most of their lives on the main sequence, a diagonal band running from hot, luminous blue stars (upper left) to cool, dim red dwarf stars (lower right). Luminosity and temperature are related for main sequence stars by the mass–luminosity relation $L \propto M^{3.5}$ and the Stefan–Boltzmann law $L = 4\pi R^2 \sigma T^4$. A star's position on the main sequence is determined by its mass.

When a star exhausts hydrogen in its core, it leaves the main sequence. For a Sun-like star: core contracts and heats → hydrogen shell burning begins → outer layers expand and cool → star becomes a red giant (upper right of HR diagram). Eventually the helium core undergoes helium flash (fusion of He → C, O via triple-alpha process: $3{}^4_2\mathrm{He} \to {}^{12}_6\mathrm{C} + \gamma$). After helium exhaustion, the outer envelope is expelled as a planetary nebula, leaving a white dwarf (lower left, very hot but dim, cooling over billions of years — no fusion). For massive stars ($M > {\sim}8\,M_\odot$): after red supergiant phase, iron core forms (iron is the most stable nucleus — no energy can be released by fusion or fission). The core collapses in milliseconds, triggering a supernova explosion. The remnant is a neutron star (very dense, ~$10$ km radius) or, for the most massive cores ($M_{\text{core}} > {\sim}3\,M_\odot$), a black hole. Supernovae are the primary source of elements heavier than iron in the universe.

Fusion as an energy source offers compelling advantages: fuel (deuterium from seawater, tritium bred from lithium) is effectively limitless; no long-lived radioactive waste (the main product is helium-4; tritium has $T_{1/2} = 12.3$ years; neutron-activated reactor components are radioactive but for far shorter times than fission waste); no risk of runaway chain reaction (if confinement fails, the plasma simply cools and fusion stops); no CO$_2$ emissions. The key engineering challenges are achieving and sustaining plasma temperatures above $10^8$ K, meeting the Lawson criterion, managing neutron damage to reactor walls, and achieving a net energy gain ($Q > 1$, ideally $Q \gg 1$ for commercial viability).

quiz.question: Explain why fusion of light nuclei releases energy, using the concept of binding energy per nucleon.
quiz.markscheme: The binding energy per nucleon ($\mathrm{BE}/A$) curve has a maximum at iron ($A \approx 56$, $\mathrm{BE}/A \approx 8.8$ MeV). For nuclei lighter than iron, $\mathrm{BE}/A$ increases as A increases. When two light nuclei fuse to form a heavier product closer to the peak, the product has a higher $\mathrm{BE}/A$ than the reactants. Because the product is more tightly bound, the total nuclear binding energy of the system is greater; this increase in binding energy equals the energy released, given by $\Delta E = \Delta m \times c^2$, where $\Delta m$ is the mass defect (mass of reactants minus mass of products). For deuterium–tritium fusion, approximately $17.6$ MeV is released per reaction.

quiz.question: State the conditions required for nuclear fusion to occur and explain why these conditions are difficult to achieve on Earth.
quiz.markscheme: Conditions: (1) Very high temperature — $T \geq {\sim}10^8$ K (about $10$ keV) so that nuclei have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the Coulomb repulsion barrier and approach within ~$10^{-15}$ m (range of strong force). (2) High plasma density and sufficient confinement time — described by the Lawson criterion: $n \tau_E \geq {\sim}10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$ s for D–T. Difficulties on Earth: no solid material can contain plasma at $10^8$ K (any contact would cool the plasma and melt the walls); magnetic confinement (tokamak) uses strong magnetic fields to keep plasma from touching walls, but sustaining stable confinement at the required density and temperature simultaneously is extremely challenging; energy breakeven ($Q \geq 1$) has not yet been achieved in a sustained manner for practical power generation.

quiz.question: Describe the proton–proton chain in the Sun and calculate the energy released when four protons fuse to form one helium-4 nucleus. ($m_p = 1.007276\,u$; $m_{\text{He-4}} = 4.002602\,u$; $m_e = 0.000549\,u$; $1\,u = 931.5$ MeV/$c^2$)
quiz.markscheme: The pp chain net reaction is: $4{}^1_1\mathrm{H} \to {}^4_2\mathrm{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu_e + 2\gamma$ (plus the annihilation energy from the two positrons meeting two electrons: $2e^+ + 2e^- \to 4\gamma$). Mass of 4 protons: $4 \times 1.007276 = 4.029104\,u$. Mass of He-4: $4.002602\,u$. Mass of 2 positrons: $2 \times 0.000549 = 0.001098\,u$. Total mass of products: $4.002602 + 0.001098 = 4.003700\,u$. Mass defect: $\Delta m = 4.029104 - 4.003700 = 0.025404\,u$. Energy: $\Delta E = 0.025404 \times 931.5 = 23.66$ MeV. Including annihilation of 2 positrons with 2 electrons: $4 \times 0.000549 \times 931.5 = 2.04$ MeV additional. Total $\approx 26.7$ MeV. Steps of the chain: (1) $\mathrm{p} + \mathrm{p} \to {}^2\mathrm{H} + e^+ + \nu_e$; (2) ${}^2\mathrm{H} + \mathrm{p} \to {}^3\mathrm{He} + \gamma$; (3) ${}^3\mathrm{He} + {}^3\mathrm{He} \to {}^4\mathrm{He} + 2\mathrm{p}$ (requiring steps 1 and 2 twice).

quiz.question: Describe the main sequence and explain what determines a star's position on the Hertzsprung–Russell diagram during the main sequence phase.
quiz.markscheme: The main sequence is a diagonal band on the HR diagram (luminosity vs. surface temperature) from upper-left (hot, luminous, blue-white O and B stars) to lower-right (cool, dim, red M dwarf stars). A star lies on the main sequence while it is fusing hydrogen to helium in its core — the longest phase of its life. Its position is determined primarily by its mass: more massive stars have higher core temperature and pressure, higher fusion rate, much higher luminosity ($L \propto M^{3.5}$), and higher surface temperature. The Stefan–Boltzmann law $L = 4\pi R^2 \sigma T^4$ links luminosity, radius, and surface temperature. More massive main-sequence stars are larger, hotter, more luminous, and are situated higher-left on the HR diagram; less massive stars are smaller, cooler, less luminous, and situated lower-right.

quiz.question: Compare the end stages of a star similar in mass to the Sun with the end stages of a star of mass $15\,M_\odot$.
quiz.markscheme: Sun-like star ($1\,M_\odot$): When hydrogen in the core is exhausted, the core contracts and heats while hydrogen shell burning expands the outer envelope → red giant. Helium core eventually reaches ~$10^8$ K, triggering helium fusion (triple-alpha: $3{}^4\mathrm{He} \to {}^{12}\mathrm{C} + \gamma$) — helium flash. After helium exhaustion, the star cannot fuse carbon (insufficient mass for higher temperatures). Outer layers are shed as a planetary nebula; the core remains as a white dwarf (electron-degenerate matter, ~Earth size, no fusion, slowly cooling). Massive star ($15\,M_\odot$): Undergoes successive fusion stages ($\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{He} \to \mathrm{C} \to \mathrm{Ne} \to \mathrm{O} \to \mathrm{Si} \to \mathrm{Fe}$) in an onion-shell structure. Iron core cannot release energy by fusion or fission (maximum $\mathrm{BE}/A$). When iron core exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (~$1.4\,M_\odot$), it collapses catastrophically in ~$0.1$ s → core bounce → supernova Type II explosion. The remnant is a neutron star (neutron-degenerate matter, ~$10$ km radius, ~$10^{17}$ kg m$^{-3}$) or, if core mass exceeds ~$3\,M_\odot$, a black hole.

quiz.question: Discuss nuclear fusion as a potential future energy source, identifying two advantages over fission and two engineering challenges that remain to be solved.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages over fission: (1) Fuel supply is essentially unlimited — deuterium is extracted from seawater (1 in ~$6400$ hydrogen atoms is deuterium; oceans contain ~$10^{43}$ deuterium nuclei), and tritium can be bred from lithium in the reactor blanket. Fission requires mining limited uranium ore. (2) Radioactive waste is far less problematic — the main fusion product is helium-4 (inert, stable). Tritium has $T_{1/2} = 12.3$ years and decays to manageable levels within decades; neutron activation of reactor components produces isotopes with half-lives of years to decades, not thousands of years as with fission waste. Engineering challenges: (1) Achieving plasma temperatures above $10^8$ K and simultaneously satisfying the Lawson criterion ($n \tau_E \geq 10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$ s) in a stable, sustained configuration — plasma instabilities (e.g. kink, sausage) disrupt confinement and quench the plasma. (2) Materials challenge — $14.1$ MeV neutrons from D–T fusion bombard the reactor wall, causing radiation damage, embrittlement, and activation (making structural materials radioactive). No material has yet been demonstrated to withstand this neutron flux for the lifetime of a commercial reactor.
